name: Wagner Group / Africa Corps — Mali
detail: Approximately 1,000–1,500 Russian paramilitaries, rebranded from Wagner Group to Africa Corps (under Russian MoD/GRU direct command) after the June 2023 Prigozhin mutiny. Arrived December 2021; deployed to Mopti, Gao, Timbuktu, Kidal, and Tessalit. Played decisive role in November 2023 Kidal offensive. Associated with numerous civilian atrocity incidents including the Moura massacre. Annual cost to Mali estimated at $10–15M plus gold mining rights.

name: Africa Corps — Burkina Faso
detail: Russia's Africa Corps (formerly Wagner) expanded to Burkina Faso following the September 2022 coup. Several hundred personnel deployed as trainers and combat advisers. Arrival coincides with French forces' expulsion in January 2023. Africa Corps in Burkina Faso has been linked to civilian killings in multiple ACLED-documented incidents.

name: Operation Serval — France (2013–2014)
detail: French counter-insurgency operation launched January 11, 2013 in response to jihadist advance on Bamako. Deployed up to 4,500 troops, Mirage and Rafale fighter jets, Tiger attack helicopters, and Reaper drones. Liberated all major northern Malian cities within 3 weeks. Ended July 15, 2014 having killed approximately 625 militants. Cost: approximately €650M. Replaced by Operation Barkhane.

name: Operation Barkhane — France (2014–2022)
detail: France's permanent counter-terrorism mission across the G5 Sahel states, headquartered in N'Djamena, Chad, with major bases in Gao (Mali) and Niamey (Niger). Peak deployment: 5,500 troops across Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad, Mauritania. Annual cost: ~€1 billion. Terminated November 9, 2022 (Macron announcement); last forces left Mali August 15, 2022 and Niger by December 2023. Killed approximately 50 French soldiers over 8 years.

name: MINUSMA — United Nations (2013–2023)
detail: UN Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali, established by UNSC Resolution 2100 (April 25, 2013). Peak strength: ~15,000 troops and personnel from 56 nations; 12 camps across northern and central Mali. Annual budget: ~$1.2 billion (most expensive UN mission). Mandate terminated June 30, 2023 at Mali junta's demand; final withdrawal December 31, 2023. Total deaths: 304 (175 malicious act) — deadliest UN mission in history.

name: EUTM Mali — European Union
detail: European Union Training Mission Mali, established January 2013 to train and advise the Malian Armed Forces (FAMa). Deployed approximately 600 EU personnel (from 27 EU member states). Suspended active training activities in 2022 after the Malian junta restricted its operational freedom following Wagner Group's arrival. Formally ended mission in Bamako May 2023; reoriented to other Sahel states.

name: Task Force Takuba — European SOF
detail: European special operations task force established in 2020 under French leadership, providing direct combat support and mentoring to Malian forces alongside Barkhane. Deployed approximately 900 personnel from 14 European nations (Sweden, Czech Republic, Denmark, Netherlands, etc.). Suspended March 2022 after Mali expelled French and Swedish forces; officially dissolved May 2022.

name: US AFRICOM — Niger (2013–2024)
detail: US Africa Command maintained approximately 1,000 troops in Niger at Air Base 101 (Niamey) and Air Base 201 (Agadez), the latter a purpose-built $110M drone and intelligence facility. Operations included Reaper drone flights tracking ISGS and JNIM leadership. Following the July 2023 coup, Niger demanded US forces withdraw; the final withdrawal was completed in August 2024, ending the US military's largest Sahel counter-terrorism platform.

name: French Forces — Chad (N'Djamena)
detail: France retains approximately 1,000 troops in Chad at the N'Djamena base, down from a peak Barkhane headquarters staff of 2,000+. Chad remains the sole AES-adjacent state maintaining French military cooperation. The N'Djamena facility hosts Mirage jets and logistics for remaining French regional operations in Côte d'Ivoire and Senegal. Chad-France relations have also shown strain since 2023.

name: G5 Sahel Joint Force (FC-G5S)
detail: Regional counter-terrorism force established in 2017, combining troops from Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad, and Mauritania across three sectors (West, Center, East) covering ~6,000 km of border zone. Peak authorized strength: 5,000 troops. Chronically underfunded; repeatedly attacked by JNIM and ISGS. Effectively dissolved after Mali (2022), Burkina Faso (December 2023), and Niger (December 2023) withdrew, with Chad and Mauritania invoking dissolution articles.

name: Russian Military Advisers — Niger
detail: Following the expulsion of US and French forces in 2023–2024, Niger signed a military cooperation agreement with Russia. Approximately 100 Russian military advisers and trainers arrived by early 2025 to support the Nigerien Armed Forces (FAN). Russia also agreed to supply arms including armored vehicles and helicopters. Niger has also engaged Iran for drone procurement alongside its Russia alignment.

name: AES Unified Military Force
detail: Alliance of Sahel States joint military command inaugurated December 20, 2025 in Bamako, integrating air, land, and intelligence assets from Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger. Backed by Russian arms agreements finalized April 2025, including combat aircraft, armored vehicles, and communications equipment. Intended as the AES's answer to the dissolved G5 Sahel Joint Force and NATO-aligned frameworks, operating independently of any Western oversight.
